Authors: Smit Kadvani; Shriya Gumber; Kriti Faujdar; Harsh Dave

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Real-time 3D object detection is a critical component for the safe operation of autonomous driving systems and robotics. While LiDAR point clouds provide accurate spatial information, processing them efficiently remains a significant challenge. Traditional methods rely on complex 3D convolutions or anchor-based paradigms that struggle to balance detection accuracy with inference speed. In this paper, we propose PillarDETR, a novel end-to-end 3D object detection architecture that combines the efficiency of pillar-based LiDAR encoding with the representational power of modern 2D vision models. Specifically, PillarDETR replaces standard convolutional backbones with a Cross Stage Partial (CSP) network derived from YOLOv8, enabling richer feature extraction from pseudoimages. Furthermore, we discard conventional anchor-based or center-based detection heads in favor of a Real-Time Detection Transformer (RT-DETR) decoder. This hybrid design allows the network to capture global context and directly predict 3D bounding boxes without relying on non-maximum suppression (NMS). Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate that PillarDETR achieves a compelling trade-off between mean Average Precision (mAP) and inference latency. Our ablation studies confirm that integrating the YOLOv8 backbone and RT-DETR head yields substantial improvements over the PointPillars baseline, establishing PillarDETR as a highly effective solution for real-time 3D perception.

item.title: Motion Estimation for Multi-Object Tracking using KalmanNet with Semantic-Independent Encoding
item.dc:creator: Jian Song
Wei Mei
Yunfeng Xu
Qiang Fu
Renke Kou
Lina Bu
Yucheng Long
item.description: Motion estimation is a crucial component in multi-object tracking (MOT), as it predicts object trajectories across video frames to reduce tracking failures and identity switches. The Kalman filter (KF), based on a linear constant-velocity model, is widely used in MOT, but its performance degrades under parameter mismatch and non-stationary object motion. To address this, we propose Semantic-Independent KalmanNet (SIKNet), a novel learning-aided Kalman filtering (LAKF) framework for MOT motion estimation. SIKNet employs a Semantic-Independent Encoder that first encodes state components based on their semantic homogeneity (e.g., position, velocity), then models cross-dependencies across heterogeneous groups, thereby mitigating error propagation from mixed semantic representations. For independent evaluation of motion estimation models and supervised training of LAKF, we built a large-scale semi-synthetic dataset from open-source MOT benchmarks, providing paired state-measurement trajectories. Experiments show that SIKNet outperforms the traditional KF and achieves higher robustness and accuracy than existing learning-aided Kalman filters. When integrated as a drop-in replacement for the KF in trackers such as SORT and ByteTrack, SIKNet improves their tracking performance, yielding an average HOTA gain of 5-8 percentage points on challenging benchmarks, including DanceTrack and SoccerNet. Code is available at ( https://github.com/SongJgit/filternet ). Published: 2026-06-01T15:28:14+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.776 (must_read)

Motion estimation is a crucial component in multi-object tracking (MOT), as it predicts object trajectories across video frames to reduce tracking failures and identity switches. The Kalman filter (KF), based on a linear constant-velocity model, is widely used in MOT, but its performance degrades under parameter mismatch and non-stationary object motion. To address this, we propose Semantic-Independent KalmanNet (SIKNet), a novel learning-aided Kalman filtering (LAKF) framework for MOT motion estimation. SIKNet employs a Semantic-Independent Encoder that first encodes state components based on their semantic homogeneity (e.g., position, velocity), then models cross-dependencies across heterogeneous groups, thereby mitigating error propagation from mixed semantic representations. For independent evaluation of motion estimation models and supervised training of LAKF, we built a large-scale semi-synthetic dataset from open-source MOT benchmarks, providing paired state-measurement trajectories. Experiments show that SIKNet outperforms the traditional KF and achieves higher robustness and accuracy than existing learning-aided Kalman filters. When integrated as a drop-in replacement for the KF in trackers such as SORT and ByteTrack, SIKNet improves their tracking performance, yielding an average HOTA gain of 5-8 percentage points on challenging benchmarks, including DanceTrack and SoccerNet. Code is available at ( https://github.com/SongJgit/filternet ).

item.title: LoFT-CLIP: Few-shot anomaly detection for railway fasteners based on large vision-language models
item.dc:creator: Bo Tang
Zhanwei Yang
Dechen Yao
Buyao Yang
Longting Chen
Ran Bi
Yongxing Zhao
Jingsong Xie
Zhibin Guo
item.description: The integrity of railway fasteners is fundamental to ensuring operational safety. Currently, visual inspection by track vehicles faces a scarcity of anomalous fastener samples, making it difficult to deploy traditional deep learning methods that rely on massive fault data. Although large vision-language models possess few-shot transfer potential to resolve data dependency, their direct application faces semantic misalignment and the loss of fine-grained features due to a lack of industrial prior knowledge. To this end, this paper proposes a few-shot anomaly detection framework based on LoFT-CLIP. First, a two-stage fine-tuning paradigm comprising “Anomaly Related Enhanced Fine-Tuning + Rail Fastener Few-Shot Detection Task Adaptation” is constructed; it utilizes filtered industrial datasets to establish an anomaly perception foundation and combines a parameter-freezing strategy for fine-tuning in the fastener domain, thereby resolving impoverished feature representation and insufficient generalization caused by data scarcity. Second, a Low-rank Prompt Adapter (LoR-PA) is designed to dynamically calibrate text prompts and suppress background redundancy via cross-modal interaction, resolving semantic misalignment during CLIP’s adaptation to the industrial domain. Finally, a Fine-grained Side Tuner (FST) is introduced to recover fine-grained structural anomalies based on structure-semantics decoupling, endowing the large model with pixel-level perception for minute defects. Experiments on a railway fastener dataset demonstrate that LoFT-CLIP achieves an image-level AUROC of 99.4% and a pixel-level F1 score of 57.1% under a 16-shot setting, significantly outperforming existing advanced methods. Published: 2026-06-01T05:43:33+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.776 (must_read)

Authors: Bo Tang; Zhanwei Yang; Dechen Yao; Buyao Yang; Longting Chen; Ran Bi; Yongxing Zhao; Jingsong Xie; Zhibin Guo

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114099

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

The integrity of railway fasteners is fundamental to ensuring operational safety. Currently, visual inspection by track vehicles faces a scarcity of anomalous fastener samples, making it difficult to deploy traditional deep learning methods that rely on massive fault data. Although large vision-language models possess few-shot transfer potential to resolve data dependency, their direct application faces semantic misalignment and the loss of fine-grained features due to a lack of industrial prior knowledge. To this end, this paper proposes a few-shot anomaly detection framework based on LoFT-CLIP. First, a two-stage fine-tuning paradigm comprising “Anomaly Related Enhanced Fine-Tuning + Rail Fastener Few-Shot Detection Task Adaptation” is constructed; it utilizes filtered industrial datasets to establish an anomaly perception foundation and combines a parameter-freezing strategy for fine-tuning in the fastener domain, thereby resolving impoverished feature representation and insufficient generalization caused by data scarcity. Second, a Low-rank Prompt Adapter (LoR-PA) is designed to dynamically calibrate text prompts and suppress background redundancy via cross-modal interaction, resolving semantic misalignment during CLIP’s adaptation to the industrial domain. Finally, a Fine-grained Side Tuner (FST) is introduced to recover fine-grained structural anomalies based on structure-semantics decoupling, endowing the large model with pixel-level perception for minute defects. Experiments on a railway fastener dataset demonstrate that LoFT-CLIP achieves an image-level AUROC of 99.4% and a pixel-level F1 score of 57.1% under a 16-shot setting, significantly outperforming existing advanced methods.

item.title: Diffusion Models in Robotics: A Survey
item.dc:creator: Xiaokang Liu
Kevin Yuchen Ma
Chen Gao
Mike Zheng Shou
item.description: Diffusion models have demonstrated outstanding performance in image and video generation over recent years. Their strong expressive power and stable training dynamics have recently motivated researchers to integrate them into robot learning. In this survey, we trace and synthesize the latest progress in diffusion-based robot learning. We begin with an in-depth overview of robotics research history and the fundamentals of diffusion models. Next, we systematically examine diffusion-based methods in robotics, including diffusion policy approaches and diffusion-based image and video synthesizer. For diffusion policy-based methods, we present a comprehensive, top-down analysis of related approaches. We then explore the application of diffusion-based image and video generation techniques in robotic tasks, covering areas such as planning and data collection. Following this, we provide a review of key benchmarks and datasets, then summarize the major objectives addressed by diffusion-based methods and analyze their common strategies. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and outline promising future directions for diffusion-based robotic learning. Through this survey, we aim to provide a thorough resource inspiring further research at the intersection of diffusion modeling and robotics. Published: 2026-06-01T08:00:19+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.775 (must_read)

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

Diffusion models have demonstrated outstanding performance in image and video generation over recent years. Their strong expressive power and stable training dynamics have recently motivated researchers to integrate them into robot learning. In this survey, we trace and synthesize the latest progress in diffusion-based robot learning. We begin with an in-depth overview of robotics research history and the fundamentals of diffusion models. Next, we systematically examine diffusion-based methods in robotics, including diffusion policy approaches and diffusion-based image and video synthesizer. For diffusion policy-based methods, we present a comprehensive, top-down analysis of related approaches. We then explore the application of diffusion-based image and video generation techniques in robotic tasks, covering areas such as planning and data collection. Following this, we provide a review of key benchmarks and datasets, then summarize the major objectives addressed by diffusion-based methods and analyze their common strategies. Finally, we discuss the current challenges and outline promising future directions for diffusion-based robotic learning. Through this survey, we aim to provide a thorough resource inspiring further research at the intersection of diffusion modeling and robotics.

item.title: On the Limits of Token Reduction for Efficient Unified Vision Language Training
item.dc:creator: Siyi Chen
Weiming Zhuang
Jingtao Li
Lingjuan Lv
item.description: Unified vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual understanding and visual generation within a single autoregressive backbone, but their joint training is computationally expensive and largely overlooked from an efficiency perspective. In this work, we study the feasibility and limits of token-reduction-based acceleration for unified VLM training. Through a systematic analysis of layerwise attention allocation, we uncover a fundamental asymmetry: visual understanding exhibits substantial late-layer visual redundancy, whereas visual generation maintains persistent dependence on image tokens across depth. Guided by this observation, we design task-specific accelerators that selectively reduce image-token computation for each objective. While these methods achieve significant efficiency gains in isolated settings, we observe a consistent synergy loss under unified training -- task-specific token dropping necessitates divergent parameter pathways and eliminates the mutual performance gains typically observed in joint optimization. Our findings suggest that efficient unified modeling requires preserving shared cross-task structures, highlighting the need for synergy-aware acceleration strategies. Project page: https://chicychen.github.io/TokenReductionUnifiedVLM/. Published: 2026-05-31T23:59:12+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Siyi Chen; Weiming Zhuang; Jingtao Li; Lingjuan Lv

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Unified vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual understanding and visual generation within a single autoregressive backbone, but their joint training is computationally expensive and largely overlooked from an efficiency perspective. In this work, we study the feasibility and limits of token-reduction-based acceleration for unified VLM training. Through a systematic analysis of layerwise attention allocation, we uncover a fundamental asymmetry: visual understanding exhibits substantial late-layer visual redundancy, whereas visual generation maintains persistent dependence on image tokens across depth. Guided by this observation, we design task-specific accelerators that selectively reduce image-token computation for each objective. While these methods achieve significant efficiency gains in isolated settings, we observe a consistent synergy loss under unified training -- task-specific token dropping necessitates divergent parameter pathways and eliminates the mutual performance gains typically observed in joint optimization. Our findings suggest that efficient unified modeling requires preserving shared cross-task structures, highlighting the need for synergy-aware acceleration strategies. Project page: https://chicychen.github.io/TokenReductionUnifiedVLM/.

item.title: Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation
item.dc:creator: WenZhang Wei
Zhipeng Gui
Dehua Peng
Tiandi Ye
Huayi Wu
item.description: The core of vision-language models lies in measuring cross-modal similarity within a unified representation space. However, most image-text matching or multi-class image classification datasets lack fine-grained cross-modal matching annotations, forcing the continuous similarity space into binary classification boundaries. This compression induces false negative samples and significantly impairs the generalization performance of cross-modal tasks. While prior research has attempted to mitigate this by modeling intra-modal ambiguity, it often overlooks inherent annotation flaws, leading to suboptimal uncertainty allocation. To address these challenges, we propose a Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation (VACSR). This approach reformulates image-text matching with fine-grained semantic scarcity as a variational inference problem. It constructs a latent space for cross-modal similarity and uses regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting to binary annotations. Experiments on image-text retrieval, domain generalization, and base-to-novel generalization demonstrate the proposed method's effectiveness and robust generalization ability. Published: 2026-05-29T08:08:15+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: WenZhang Wei; Zhipeng Gui; Dehua Peng; Tiandi Ye; Huayi Wu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

The core of vision-language models lies in measuring cross-modal similarity within a unified representation space. However, most image-text matching or multi-class image classification datasets lack fine-grained cross-modal matching annotations, forcing the continuous similarity space into binary classification boundaries. This compression induces false negative samples and significantly impairs the generalization performance of cross-modal tasks. While prior research has attempted to mitigate this by modeling intra-modal ambiguity, it often overlooks inherent annotation flaws, leading to suboptimal uncertainty allocation. To address these challenges, we propose a Variational Adapter for Cross-modal Similarity Representation (VACSR). This approach reformulates image-text matching with fine-grained semantic scarcity as a variational inference problem. It constructs a latent space for cross-modal similarity and uses regularization techniques to mitigate overfitting to binary annotations. Experiments on image-text retrieval, domain generalization, and base-to-novel generalization demonstrate the proposed method's effectiveness and robust generalization ability.

item.title: SGA-SAC: Fusing Bayesian Stackelberg games to DRL with graph attention for explainable task offloading in vehicular edge computing
item.dc:creator: Xinmin Cheng
Binxin Xu
Hongwei Yin
Shigen Shen
Zhiquan Liu
Tian Wang
Ruidong Li
item.description: Relying solely on local computing has been unable to meet the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) requirements of low latency and high energy efficiency. To solve this issue, we propose a task offloading and resource allocation strategy for joint optimization of energy consumption and latency, which allows intelligent vehicles (IVs) to offload part of computing tasks to roadside units (RSUs) to realize the collaborative optimization of task offloading and resource scheduling. In order to describe the dynamic competition and decision-making interaction between IVs and RSUs, the system is modeled as a hierarchical Bayesian Stackelberg game model, in which RSUs are the leader and IVs are the followers. Theoretical analysis shows that there is a Nash equilibrium in the model, which provides a feasibility guarantee for the system to achieve the theoretical optimal or suboptimal strategy. On this basis, we design a deep reinforcement learning algorithm of Stackelberg Graph Attention-Soft Actor-Critic (SGA-SAC). By introducing the graph attention mechanism into the policy network and combining soft policy iteration, agents are guided to adaptively converge to efficient decisions through online exploration. In order to further improve the decision interpretability, we use the gradient weighted class activation mapping method to visualize the multi-agent decision to enhance the transparency of the system. Ablation and comparison experiments show that the proposed algorithm can effectively reduce task processing latency and system energy consumption, demonstrating obvious performance advantages in the vehicular edge computing scenario of IoV. Published: 2026-06-01T16:03:21+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.775 (must_read)

Relying solely on local computing has been unable to meet the Internet of Vehicles (IoV) requirements of low latency and high energy efficiency. To solve this issue, we propose a task offloading and resource allocation strategy for joint optimization of energy consumption and latency, which allows intelligent vehicles (IVs) to offload part of computing tasks to roadside units (RSUs) to realize the collaborative optimization of task offloading and resource scheduling. In order to describe the dynamic competition and decision-making interaction between IVs and RSUs, the system is modeled as a hierarchical Bayesian Stackelberg game model, in which RSUs are the leader and IVs are the followers. Theoretical analysis shows that there is a Nash equilibrium in the model, which provides a feasibility guarantee for the system to achieve the theoretical optimal or suboptimal strategy. On this basis, we design a deep reinforcement learning algorithm of Stackelberg Graph Attention-Soft Actor-Critic (SGA-SAC). By introducing the graph attention mechanism into the policy network and combining soft policy iteration, agents are guided to adaptively converge to efficient decisions through online exploration. In order to further improve the decision interpretability, we use the gradient weighted class activation mapping method to visualize the multi-agent decision to enhance the transparency of the system. Ablation and comparison experiments show that the proposed algorithm can effectively reduce task processing latency and system energy consumption, demonstrating obvious performance advantages in the vehicular edge computing scenario of IoV.

item.title: DarkVesselNet: Multi-Modal Remote Sensing and Trajectory Reasoning for Dark Vessel Detection
item.dc:creator: Arun Sharma
item.description: Dark vessel detection requires fusing what vessels report through AIS with what satellites observe through radar and optical sensors. DarkVesselNet is a multi-modal remote sensing stack that combines Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery, geospatial foundation model backbones, AIS trajectory reasoning, TGARD-style gap detection, and a Pi-DPM-inspired anomaly head. The repository exposes the system as a tested Python package and a public Hugging Face Space. The paper presents the sensor stack, backbone abstraction, fusion path, anomaly head, and current validation. The evidence currently available is software-grounded: tests for SAR speckle filtering, optical band ratios, Haversine distance, TGARD gap emission, sensor coregistration, backbone token shapes, and differentiable anomaly scoring. Published: 2026-05-30T00:31:06+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Arun Sharma

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Dark vessel detection requires fusing what vessels report through AIS with what satellites observe through radar and optical sensors. DarkVesselNet is a multi-modal remote sensing stack that combines Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 optical imagery, geospatial foundation model backbones, AIS trajectory reasoning, TGARD-style gap detection, and a Pi-DPM-inspired anomaly head. The repository exposes the system as a tested Python package and a public Hugging Face Space. The paper presents the sensor stack, backbone abstraction, fusion path, anomaly head, and current validation. The evidence currently available is software-grounded: tests for SAR speckle filtering, optical band ratios, Haversine distance, TGARD gap emission, sensor coregistration, backbone token shapes, and differentiable anomaly scoring.

item.title: Self-Improving Small Object Grounding in LVLMs
item.dc:creator: Tianze Yang
Yucheng Shi
Ruitong Sun
Ninghao Liu
Jin Sun
item.description: Can internal attention patterns in Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) identify reliable small-object boxes without fine-tuning? In this work, we provide an affirmative answer. Attention structure in LVLMs encodes grounding quality-a lightweight IoU regressor trained solely on attention maps achieves strong IoU prediction (Pearson r > 0.67). This regressor powers the regressor-based variant of our Attention-based Candidate Selection (ACS) framework, called ACS-Learned, which selects the best box from multiple sampled candidates to improve object grounding. By analyzing what the regressor learns, we reveal which transformer layers and heads are most critical and derive ACS-Free: a training-free selector that ranks candidates by attention entropy on these discriminative heads, with no learned component at inference. Experiments on COCO and Objects365 demonstrate up to 19% self-improvement on small object localization, with ACS-Free ranking best among all training-free methods, demonstrating that useful attention structure improves both localization reliability and interpretability in LVLMs. Published: 2026-06-01T03:01:38+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tianze Yang; Yucheng Shi; Ruitong Sun; Ninghao Liu; Jin Sun

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Can internal attention patterns in Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) identify reliable small-object boxes without fine-tuning? In this work, we provide an affirmative answer. Attention structure in LVLMs encodes grounding quality-a lightweight IoU regressor trained solely on attention maps achieves strong IoU prediction (Pearson r > 0.67). This regressor powers the regressor-based variant of our Attention-based Candidate Selection (ACS) framework, called ACS-Learned, which selects the best box from multiple sampled candidates to improve object grounding. By analyzing what the regressor learns, we reveal which transformer layers and heads are most critical and derive ACS-Free: a training-free selector that ranks candidates by attention entropy on these discriminative heads, with no learned component at inference. Experiments on COCO and Objects365 demonstrate up to 19% self-improvement on small object localization, with ACS-Free ranking best among all training-free methods, demonstrating that useful attention structure improves both localization reliability and interpretability in LVLMs.
